--- SOURCE 2: Economic losses from weather- and climate-related extremes ... ---
URL: https://www.eea.europa.eu/en/analysis/indicators/economic-losses-from-climate-related

SUMMARY:
This indicator considers estimated values for the number of fatalities, the overall and insured economic losses from weather- and climate-related events in the EEA member countries, i.e., in the 27 EU Member States and in Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, Switzerland and Türkiye. Further details are provided on the Climate-ADAPT dashboard presenting information on total economic losses, insured economic losses and fatalities for the EU-27 and for all member countries of the European Environment Agency per country, per year and per hazard type. In 2024, the European Commission presented the Communication on Managing Climate Risks-Protecting People and Prosperity which sets out how the EU can effectively get ahead of the growing climate-related risks and build greater resilience to the impacts of climate change including to address economic losses.

Economic losses from weather- and climate-related extremes in Europe
====================================================================

Published 14 Oct 2024

Share

1.   [![Image 5](https://www.eea.europa.eu/static/media/home-icon.b75040c2.svg)](https://www.eea.europa.eu/en "Home")
2.    [Analysis and data](https://www.eea.europa.eu/en/analysis)
3.    [Indicators](https://www.eea.europa.eu/en/analysis/indicators)
4.    Economic losses from weather- and climate-related extremes in Europe 

Weather- and climate-related extremes caused economic losses of assets estimated at EUR 738 billion during 1980 - 2023 in the European Union, with over EUR 162 billion (22%) between 2021 and 2023. Analysing trends in economic losses is challenging, primarily due to large annual variability. Statistical analyses revealed, that economic losses increase over time and the last three years are all in the top five of years of highest annual economic losses. As severe weather- and climate-related extreme events are expected to intensify further, it seems unlikely that associated economic losses will reduce by 2030.

### [](https://www.eea.europa.eu/en/analysis/indicators/economic-losses-from-climate-related#figure-1-annual-economic-losses-caused-by-weather-and-climate-related-extreme-events-in-the-eu-member-states)Figure 1. Annual economic losses caused by weather-and climate-related extreme events in the EU Member States

![Image 6](https://www.eea.europa.eu/en/analysis/indicators/economic-losses-from-climate-related/annual-economic-losses-caused/@@images/preview_image-900-f075ce899237051585709127e9e3f928.svg)

Sources

[More info](https://www.eea.europa.eu/en/analysis/indicators/economic-losses-from-climate-related/annual-economic-losses-caused)

Share

Download

Enlarge

Climate-related **hazards**, such as temperature extremes, heavy precipitation and droughts, pose risks to human health and ecosystems and can lead to[substantial **economic losses**](https://www.eea.europa.eu/en/analysis/indicators/economic-losses-from-climate-related/economic-losses-and-fatalities). These losses equally create pressures on public finances.

The 2021[EU Adaptation Strategy](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=COM:2021:82:FIN)aims to build **resilience** and ensure that the EU is well prepared to manage these risks. It also adapts to the impacts of climate change. The EU aims, to ultimately reduce the overall monetary losses from weather- and climate-related events.

Between 1980 and 2023, climate-related **extremes** amounted to an estimated EUR 738 billion (2023 prices) in the EU. Hydrological hazards (floods) account for 44% and meteorological hazards (storms, including lightning and hail) for almost 29% of the total. For the climatological hazards, heat waves cause almost 19% of the total losses (but are responsible for 95% of the fatalities) while the remaining 8% are caused by droughts, forest fires and cold waves together.

Relatively few events are **responsible** for most of the economic losses: 5% of climate-related events with the biggest losses are responsible for 61% of losses, and 1% of the events cause 28% of losses. However, 66% of events with the smallest losses recorded total only 5% of the losses (calculations based on the original dataset). The total losses vary significantly from year to year. This interannual variability is due to the development of assets in vulnerable areas and potential reporting bias over time and because most types of weather- and climate-related extremes across the world have become more severe and frequent as a result of human-caused climate change.

The average **annua****l** (constant 2023 EUR prices) economic losses were around EUR 8.5 billion in 1980-1989, 14.0 billion in 1990-1999, 15.8 billion in 2000-2009, 17.8 billion in 2010-2019 and 44.5 billion for the period 2020-2023. A statistical analysis of a 30-year moving average reveals that economic losses increased over time. A linear trendline through these 30-year averages represent a 53% increase from 2009 to 2023, or 2.9% per year.

The five years with highest annual values are:

*   2021 (EUR 63.0 billion);
*   2022 (56.0 billion);
*   2002 (45.7 billion);
*   2023 (43.9 billion);
*   1999 (36.7 billion).

The[Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change](https://www.ipcc.ch/)predicts that climate-related extreme events will become more frequent and severe around the world. This affects multiple sectors and causes **systemic failures** across Europe, creating greater economic losses.

The first [European Climate Risk Assessment](https://www.eea.europa.eu/publications/european-climate-risk-assessment) concluded that **climate risks** are accelerating. Several of the 36 key climate risks are at critical levels and of high urgency. Climate-related extreme events are expected to intensify further and the adaptation pace is not following the same speed . It seems unlikely, although uncertain, that the EU will be able to mitigate the impact of these events by building resilience and the associated economic losses will reduce by 2030.

The future cost of climate-related hazards depends on the frequency and severity of events and several other factors, such as the value of the assets exposed and the effectiveness of the implemented climate adaptation measures. Studies show the **benefits of adaptation** measures, including nature-based solutions, to mitigate the impacts of weather- and climate-related extremes in Europe. A comprehensive, integrated approach is required to adapt to and manage the risks, and develop strategies that deal with the remaining and residual risks not mitigated by adaptation measures.

Enhancing society’s resilience to climate change through a focus to increasing **adaptive capacity** is key to the[EU’s adaptation strategy](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=COM:2021:82:FIN). If fully implemented the strategy, together with national regional and local strategies and plans, can contribute to limiting the economic costs of weather- and climate-related events and close the climate protection gap. An example of such an activity coordinated by the European Commission is the Climate Resilience Dialogue.

The economic impact of climate-related extremes varies across the EU. In absolute terms, the **highest** economic losses in the period 1980-2023 were gauged in Germany, Italy and France. Highest losses per capita were reckoned in Slovenia, Luxembourg and Italy, and the highest losses per area (in km 2) were in Slovenia, Belgium, and Germany.

According to estimates, less than 20% of total losses were (privately) insured. This varied among countries, from less than 2% in Romania, Slovenia, Cyprus and Bulgaria to over 35% in Denmark, Luxembourg, Belgium, the Netherlands and France. There were significant **differences** between the types of events. For meteorological events, over one-third of the losses were insured. It was less than 15% for hydrological events and slightly over 10% for all climatological events, including heatwaves, droughts and forest fires.

The[EU adaptation strategy](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=COM:2021:82:FIN)aims to promote action at **national level**. All countries have a national adaptation policy adopted using different instruments such as strategies and national, regional and sectoral plans, also laws with adaptation relevance reflecting differences in governance in between countries. The[Climate-ADAPT platform](https://climate-adapt.eea.europa.eu/)— developed by the European Commission and the EEA — supports action by sharing knowledge on climate change and its impacts, adaptation strategies and plans,and case studies.

No coherent mechanism is currently in place for countries to report losses to the European Commission or the EEA. This could be a key element under development as part of the implementation of the 'smarter adaptation' objective of the[EU adaptation strategy](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=COM:2021:82:FIN).

### Supporting information

Definition 

This indicator considers estimated values for the number of fatalities, the overall and insured economic losses from weather- and climate-related events in the EEA member countries, i.e., in the 27 EU Member States and in Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, Switzerland and Türkiye. Focus of the indicator is on total economic losses for the EU-27. Further details are provided on the [Climate-ADAPT dashboard](https://climate-adapt.eea.europa.eu/en/knowledge/economic-losses/economic-losses-and-fatalities) presenting information on total economic losses, insured economic losses and fatalities for the EU-27 and for all member countries of the European Environment Agency per country, per year and per hazard type. Hazards considered are those classified as meteorological hazards, hydrological hazards and climatological hazards, based on the classification by the International Council for Science (ICSU).

Methodology 

Data have been adjusted to account for inflation. They are presented in 2023 prices (Euro). The implicit GDP deflator is used as an economic metric that measures the price level changes of all new, final goods and services produced in an economy over a specific period, relative to the base year, including those that are not included in the consumer price index (CPI), such as investment goods and exports. As the CPI only reflects the price changes of a specific basket of goods and services that consumers purchase, the implicit GDP deflator is a more comprehensive measure of price changes than the CPI.

Definition of a loss event: the event can occur in several countries; events are counted by country and by year and type of natural hazard. The 30-year moving averages are based on the value of the year and the 29 preceding years. The estimated annual increase over the period from 2009 to 2023 is based on a linear trendline determined with the least squares method.

The European Commission is working with Member States, the ISDR and other international organisations to improve data on disaster losses. The JRC, with the [disaster risk management knowledge centre](https://drmkc.jrc.ec.europa.eu/) and the [risk data hub](https://drmkc.jrc.ec.europa.eu/risk-data-hub#/), has prepared guidance for recording and sharing disaster damage and loss data, status and best practices for disaster loss data recording in EU Member States and recommendations for a European approach for recording disaster losses. Once comparable national databases on disaster losses are available for all EU Member States and EEA member countries and these data are reported, this EEA indicator can build on such data.

**Data sources & providers**

This assessment is based on the estimates provided by the RiskLayer CATDAT dataset (dataset url is not available) and the Eurostat collection of economic indicators, whereas data from earlier years not covered by Eurostat have been completed using data from the Annual Macro-Economic Database of the European Commission (AMECO), the International Monetary Fund’s (IMF) World Economic Outlook (WEO), the Total Economy Database (TED) and the World Bank database.

Data are received from the RiskLayer CATDAT under institutional agreement.

#### Methodology for gap filling

Data gap filling is not necessary.

Policy/environmental relevance 

In February 2021, the European Commission presented the new [EU Strategy on adaptation to climate change](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=COM:2021:82:FIN). One of the objectives is 'smarter adaptation', within which a key action is 'more and better climate-related risk and losses data'. This is further developed in the Staff Working Document, Closing the climate protection gap - scoping policy and data gaps and in the activities of the [Climate Resilience Dialogue](https://climate.ec.europa.eu/eu-action/adaptation-climate-change/climate-resilience-dialogue_en), publishing its final report in July 2024. In 2024, the European Commission presented the [Communication on Managing Climate Risks-Protecting People and Prosperity](https://climate.ec.europa.eu/eu-action/adaptation-climate-change/managing-climate-risks-protecting-people-and-prosperity_en) which sets out how the EU can effectively get ahead of the growing climate-related risks and build greater resilience to the impacts of climate change including to address economic losses. It responded to the first ever [European Climate Risk Assessment Report.](https://www.eea.europa.eu/publications/european-climate-risk-assessment)

Article 6 of the [European Union Civil Protection Mechanism](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32013D1313) (2013) (EUCPM) obliges the EU Member States to develop risk assessments at national or appropriate sub-national levels and to make a summary of the relevant elements thereof. The [amendment of the EUCPM of March 2019](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32019D0420) introduced joint reporting on national risk assessments, risk management capability assessments and information on the priority prevention and preparedness measures. This with a focus on key risks with cross-border impacts, and, where appropriate, low probability risks with a high impact.

The[Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction](https://www.undrr.org/publication/sendai-framework-disaster-risk-reduction-2015-2030)(2015-2030), including ‘Understanding disaster risk’, requires that the signatory countries systematically evaluate, record, share and publicly account for disaster losses and understand the economic impacts at national and sub-national levels.

This indicator is an [EU indicator for the sustainable development goals](https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/web/sdi) (SDGs, for SDG13 Climate) and a headline indicator for monitoring progress towards the 8th Environment Action Programme. It contributes to monitoring aspects of the 8 th EAP priority objective Article 2.2. b that shall be met by 2030: ‘continuous progress in enhancing and mainstreaming adaptive capacity, including on the basis of ecosystem approaches, strengthening resilience and adaptation and reducing the vulnerability of the environment, society and all sectors of the economy to climate change, while improving prevention of, and preparedness for, weather- and climate-related disasters’. The European Commission Communication on the 8th EAP monitoring framework specifies that this indicator should be used to monitor whether the EU is reducing the overall monetary losses from weather- and climate-related events.

**Targets**

No targets have been identified for this indicator.

Accuracy and uncertainties 

No uncertainties have been specified.

Abstract
--------

Existing studies on extreme event attribution are typically event-specific, particularly those conducted since the early 2000s. They primarily focus on direct economic losses to people and physical assets while overlooking the broader indirect losses that ripple through supply chains. Here, we combine disaster data with attribution studies and employ a global multi-regional input‒output model to assess both direct and indirect economic losses from droughts, floods, and storms attributable to anthropogenic climate change between 2009 and 2019. Our findings reveal that such extreme events cause 60.32 billion USD in direct economic losses and 65.07 billion USD in indirect economic losses annually—together accounting for about 46% of total losses from all extreme events. Climate adaptation investments are urgently needed in sectors such as manufacturing, agriculture, construction, transportation, and finance, with vulnerable regions concentrated in parts of Asia. International trade and industry linkages amplify the domestic impact of international extreme events, with 17% of China's attributable losses stemming from disasters abroad. This suggests that overseas adaptation could yield domestic resilience benefits. This study underscores the interconnected nature of global economic resilience against climate change and supports the design of equitable, science-based international climate finance mechanisms.

1. Introduction
---------------

Human-induced climate change has markedly increased the frequency and intensity of extreme events. Alongside slow-onset changes such as rising sea levels and the long-term decline in precipitation, these extreme events pose escalating global risks ([IPCC, 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib19)). While slow-onset changes evolve gradually ([Kotz et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib25); [Waidelich et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib55)), extreme events—such as heatwaves, floods, storms, droughts, and wildfires—occur suddenly and often lead to acute regional impacts. Both slow-onset and extreme events result in huge economic losses, and these losses can be divided into two types. Direct losses are linked to the initial disruption to economic flows caused by these events, including the destruction of infrastructure and disruptions to production. Indirect economic losses, meanwhile, arise as subsequent or secondary results of the initial destruction; this can include supply chain interruptions and labor market instability. The International Disaster Database reports that the annual average of global direct economic losses from extreme events exceeded 210 billion USD between 2010 and 2023 ([CRED, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib10)), while the United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction ([UNDRR, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib53)) notes a 151% increase in economic losses from extreme events between 1998 and 2017. This trend underscores the increasing influence of human activities on the probability, severity, and magnitude of extreme events ([Bilal and Känzig, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib4); [Stott, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib49)), which is further confirmed by the IPCC's Sixth Assessment Report ([IPCC, 2023b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib20)).

The losses and damage caused by climate change have become the central focus of international climate negotiations, particularly following the establishment of the Loss and Damage Fund at COP27 and its formal launch at COP28 under the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC). These developments highlight the urgent need for effective mechanisms to address the disproportionate impacts of climate-related extreme events on vulnerable developing countries ([Liu et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib29)). A key challenge in advancing this mechanism, however, lies in addressing the lack of consensus on how to quantify the economic losses attributable to anthropogenic climate change, which complicates determining financial needs and guiding the equitable allocations of funds.

The recently developed method of extreme event attribution (EEA) quantifies how climate change has altered the occurrence of observed extreme events ([Noy et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib38)). Over 600 EEA studies using risk-based probabilistic methods ([Allen, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib2); [Otto et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib43)) and process-based storyline methods ([Takayabu et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib51)) have been conducted as of 2024 ([McSweeney and Tandon, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib32)). Extreme event impact attribution (EEIA) further advances attribution science by linking the occurrence of extreme events to associated social and economic losses, thus providing valuable insights for climate change adaptation as well as loss and damage assessment. Previous research has attributed the economic impacts of extreme events such as heatwaves ([Mitchell et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib35)), floods ([Dalagnol et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib11)), droughts ([Frame et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib13)), and storms ([Frame et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib14); [Li and Otto, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib26)), ultimately attributing a huge portion of this impact to anthropogenic climate change.

However, previous EEIA studies have primarily focused on direct economic losses to people and physical assets, such as mortality, damage to agricultural production (_e.g_., droughts destroying crops), infrastructure (_e.g_., floods damaging power lines), and exposed assets (_e.g_., typhoons destroying homes) ([Clarke et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib8); [Piontek et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib45)) while often overlooking the indirect losses that ripple through the entire supply chain. The complexity of the transmission of economic impacts and the diversity of methodologies have led to varied definitions and estimates of these indirect losses ([Chen et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib6); [Jin et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib22)). For example, disaster footprint models consider the post-disaster recovery needs across all industry sectors and households ([Mendoza-Tinoco et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib33); [Sun et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib50)), while input‒output (IO) models primarily examine how losses propagate through industrial connections ([Chen et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib6)). Despite these methodology differences, it is widely acknowledged that indirect losses form a substantial proportion of the impact of disasters ([Sun et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib50); [Wei et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib56)). However, estimates for the attributable indirect losses due to extreme events remain scarce, which contributes to a substantial underestimation of the full impacts of climate change.

Loss and damage, as well as adaptation, are particularly critical for developing countries and those vulnerable to climate change, where extreme events often severely affect health and livelihoods ([Arora et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib3); [Gough et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib15)). Economic constraints in the developing country context make basic needs more susceptible to disasters. Inadequate infrastructure and limited resources exacerbate impacts, while low insurance coverage hinders recovery. For instance, [Smiley et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib48) found that low-income Latina communities experience more severe impacts from flood events, and [Sun et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib50) showed that small- and medium-sized developing countries suffer disproportionately from health impacts and reduced labor productivity due to global heatwaves. Limited disaster data collection and reporting capabilities in these regions often led to underreported impacts. EEIA studies, however, have predominantly focused on Asia, the Americas, and Europe—especially middle- and high-income countries—while often overlooking the climate impacts that are severely affecting the Global South.

This study provides a comprehensive global evaluation of both direct and indirect economic losses from extreme events attributable to climate change to address the lack of attribution-based estimates for indirect losses and the insufficient attention to vulnerable developing countries in global assessments. By integrating advances in attribution science with a global multi-regional IO (MRIO) model, we trace these losses from the initial capital stock damages to their cascading effects across sectors and regions. This multi-regional, multi-hazard, and multi-sectoral attribution of historical disaster losses offers new insights into systematic approaches for adapting to the adverse impacts of climate change, thereby enhancing our understanding of the economic dimensions of extreme events. It thus helps to bridge methodological gaps and inform more equitable and science-based international climate finance mechanisms.

2. Methods and data sources
---------------------------

This study follows the framework proposed by [Okuyama and Santos (2014)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib42) to define the direct and indirect economic losses due to extreme events and distinguishes three types of impacts: damages, losses, and higher-order effects. Damages refer to the physical destruction of capital assets—such as buildings, infrastructure, and machinery. These damages impair the capacity of affected sectors to produce and consume; they also further propagate through upstream and downstream supply chains, resulting in broader higher-order effects ([Rose, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib46)). In this framework, damages are stock measures, while both losses and higher-order effects are flow measures. Because stock and flow metrics are not directly additive, we adopt a flow-based definition for both direct and indirect losses. More specifically, direct losses are defined as the immediate reductions in economic flows—such as output or consumption—in sectors directly affected by capital stock damage. These disruptions can cascade through economic links across regions and industries to generate broader total output losses. The indirect losses are then calculated as the difference between total output losses and direct losses, thus capturing the systemic and interconnected consequences of the initial shock. This flow-based distinction is well-established in IO disaster impact assessments ([Koks and Thissen, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib23); [Okuyama, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib39); [Okuyama and Sahin, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib41)) and contrasts with adaptive regional IO (ARIO)-based models that classify direct losses as stock damages and indirect losses as recovery-phase flow reductions ([Hallegatte, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib16)). Given that our focus is on the cascading consequences of initial shocks—rather than recovery dynamics—the flow-based approach is more appropriate for our analysis.

This study employs a three-step methodology to quantify both the direct and indirect economic losses from extreme events attributable to anthropogenic climate change ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#fig1)). In Step 1, we compile data on the direct economic damages from extreme events at the global scale. Using climate attribution studies, we then decompose these damages into two components: the portion attributable to anthropogenic climate change and the portion attributable to natural climate variability. In Step 2, we allocate the attributable direct damage to specific economic sectors based on their exposure profiles. In Step 3, we convert these initial stock losses into flow-based direct losses using a stock-to-flow transformation. Then, we employ a global IO model to estimate indirect losses, thus capturing how direct disruptions propagate through interlinked economic systems. By combining sectoral direct losses with the resulting indirect losses, the analysis provides a comprehensive, system-wide estimate of the global economic impacts of extreme events attributable to anthropogenic climate change.

### 2.1. Attributing the economic damage from disasters to climate change

The EEA methodology was developed to assess the contribution of climate change to the risk of extreme events by comparing the probability of an observed event with that in a counterfactual scenario without climate change. Scientists use climate models to simulate the probability of specific extreme events under two different climate conditions: the current scenario (with anthropogenic climate change) and a counterfactual scenario (without anthropogenic climate change). As illustrated in [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#fig2), extreme events are identified when climate variables (_e.g_., temperature, precipitation) exceed a certain threshold. Comparing these distributions make it possible to determine the contribution of anthropogenic climate change, which is quantified as the fraction of attributable risk (FAR) and is given by(1)FAR=1−P 0 P 1 where P 0 represents the probability of an extreme event occurring without anthropogenic climate change, and P 1 represents the probability under current climate conditions (including anthropogenic climate change). A FAR of 1 indicates that the event is entirely attributable to anthropogenic climate change, while a FAR of 0 suggests no impact from anthropogenic climate change.

The FAR concept can be extended to the attribution of economic impacts, which leads to the impact fraction of attributable risk (IFAR) methodology, which is expressed as Impact × FAR. For example, if the estimated FAR is 0.5, it can be inferred that, out of 10 million USD in losses from a flood disaster, 5 million USD are attributable to anthropogenic climate change, as shown in Eq. [(2)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#fd2) ([Brown, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib5)).(2)Attributable cost=FAR×Economic cost

The IFAR method has been applied to attribute the human and economic impacts of events such as heatwaves and other disasters ([Clarke et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib7); [Frame et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib13); [Mitchell et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib35); [Newman and Noy, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib37)). It is a suitable approach for attributing the impacts of disasters involving multiple hazard types over long-term scales and across different geographical locations.

### 2.2. Allocating direct economic damage to different sectors

Estimating the indirect effects and the distribution of disaster losses in the socio-economic system requires knowledge of the sectoral and regional breakdown of direct losses incurred by extreme events. These disaggregated directly affected sectors can be considered as an exogenous shock to the IO model to derive indirect economic losses. Typically, disaster statistics provide only the overall direct damage for each disaster, which calls for a method to disaggregate the overall direct economic damage to specific sectors.

We first identify the sectors most directly affected by disasters by reviewing the literature and statistical data, as summarized in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#tbl1), and we found consistent evidence across sources regarding the primary sectors affected by each disaster type. More specifically, droughts most severely affect agriculture, manufacturing, transportation, electricity, and water; floods and storms typically impose direct physical damage on sectors like transportation, water facilities, construction, and manufacturing.

Table 1. Allocation of directly affected sectors by disaster type.

| Disaster type | Main reference | Directly affected sector |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Drought | U.S. [NDRP (2025)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib36): drought affects infrastructure, including water supply, electricity supply, transport, and shipping [UNDRR (2021a)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib52): drought affects agricultural production, public water supply, energy production, water transport, and communications | Agriculture, manufacturing, transport, electricity, water facilities |
| Flood | [CMA (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib9): total agriculture, industry, information and transport, water facilities, and other direct economic losses Floodinfo (Irish government): human health, transport, electricity, gas, and water supply Washington state government: loss of human life, property and infrastructure damage, transport, crop destruction and livestock loss, water contamination, housing displacement | Agriculture, mining, manufacturing, construction, transport, electricity, water facilities |
| Storm | [Yan et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib59): agriculture, public facilities, manufacturing, transport, shipping, residential, tourism [Liu et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib27): agriculture, transport, water conservancy facilities | Agriculture, manufacturing, construction, transport, electricity, water facilities |

It is important to note that the sector classifications employed in IO modeling are based on standardized industrial sectors as defined in national economic accounts. These classifications are crucial for quantifying economic transactions and interdependencies between industries. They do not, however, always align with the categories commonly employed in international adaptation policy frameworks, such as those outlined in nationally determined contributions and national adaptation plans. Policy-driven classifications—including sectors like agriculture, coastal zone management, health, infrastructure, water resources, and ecosystems—often span multiple industrial sectors in economic models. This discrepancy underscores the need to develop integrated classification methodologies that bridge the gap between economic modeling and policy-oriented sectoral analyses.

We next quantify the magnitude of direct damage in each economic sector. Disasters typically cause economic damage by damaging physical assets. In disaster risk management, capital stock is a key indicator of sectoral economic exposure to natural disasters ([Wu et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib57), [2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib58)). We estimate direct losses for the agriculture sector using data from the Food and Agriculture Organization ([FAO, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib12)), which provides the average proportions of sector-specific losses from various natural disasters. On average, the agricultural sector accounts for 84% of total losses from droughts, 18% from storms, and 15% from floods. For other sectors, we allocate direct damage to sectors based on the share of capital stock each sector holds in the base year within each region ([Liu et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib28)). We also employ a flow-based allocation approach for sensitivity analysis using sectoral value-added as the indicator. The results are shown in Appendix Text A3. It is important to note that while our method provides a reasonable estimation for the distribution of direct losses at the sectoral level, it is less precise for the agriculture sector, because the FAO's estimates are primarily sourced from surveys in low- and middle-income countries and thus may not fully reflect regional disparities. It is, however, an acceptable approximation in the absence of more detailed data on the impact of disasters on agriculture.

### 2.3. Assessing indirect losses based on MRIO

To quantify the indirect economic losses from extreme events, we develop a global MRIO model based on a classical demand-driven framework. While ARIO models are widely used in disaster impact analysis ([Sun et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674927825001509#bib50)) and incorporate post-disaster adaptive behaviors such as input substitution and supply chain rebalancing, they are not suited to our objectives. ARIO models focus on both immediate impacts and recovery dynamics, which vary considerably across global regions and require event-specific assumptions. # New report: extreme weather events cost economy $2 trillion over the last decade

A new report, commissioned by the International Chamber of Commerce, estimates that climate-related extreme weather events have cost the global economy more than $2 trillion over the past decade.

![](https://iccwbo.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2024/11/2024-ICC-Oxera-The-economic-cost-of-extreme-weather-events-cover.png?w=250&h=340&crop=1)
![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)

## Read the Oxera study results

![](https://iccwbo.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2024/11/2024-ICC-Oxera-The-economic-cost-of-extreme-weather-events-cover.png?w=250&h=340&crop=1)
![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)

## Read the Oxera study results

![](https://iccwbo.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2024/11/2024_ICC_COP29_web-banner_Oxera.png?w=1080&h=1085&crop=1)
![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)

Go directly to:

Share this:

![](https://iccwbo.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2024/11/2024_ICC_COP29_SMposts_oxera.png?w=819)

The research – conducted by the specialist economics consultancy Oxera – pinpoints almost 4,000 events which impacted a total 1.6 billion people between 2014 and 2023.

In the last two full years alone, global economic damages reached $451 billion – representing a 19% increase compared to the previous eight years of the decade. The analysis by Oxera also highlights the acute impact on many developing economies –with single extreme weather events often imposing economic costs more than a country’s annual GDP.

![](https://iccwbo.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2024/02/Denton-John.png?w=500&h=330&crop=1)
![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)

The data from the past decade shows definitively that climate change is not a future problem: major productivity losses from extreme weather events are being felt in the here and now by the real economy.

![](https://iccwbo.org/wp-content/themes/icc/dist/images/default-1800-330.jpg)
![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)

Our latest analysis underscores the substantial economic toll of climate-related extreme weather events, with an estimated $2 trillion in global losses from nearly 4,000 events—a figure that likely underestimates the true scale of the impact. These events are not only reshaping economies but are deepening inequalities worldwide, placing a significant strain on both public resources and private assets.

The new report was published at the start of COP29 in Baku, with the global business organisation pointing to the findings of the study as evidence of the need for greater urgency from governments in taking action to stem climate change.

Mr Denton added: **“The upcoming UN Climate Change Conference cannot be – as some have suggested – a ‘transitional’ COP. We need to see outcomes capable of accelerating climate action commensurate with the immediate economic risks. This must start with a comprehensive package to accelerate the deployment of finance to ensure that all countries can transition towards low-carbon and climate-resilient development without further delay.**

**“Financing climate action in the developing world shouldn’t be seen as an act of generosity by the leaders of the world’s richest economies. Every dollar spent is, ultimately, an investment in a stronger and more resilient global economy from which we all benefit.**

**“From a business perspective, the urgency of coordinated and collective action to accelerate emissions reductions and build resilience to changing weather patterns cannot be overstated. Simply put, the time for action is now.”**

[Find out more](https://iccwbo.org/global-insights/sustainability-and-climate-action/) about ICC’s work on sustainability and climate action.

Climate inaction comes at a high price to us all.

NOAA’s National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI) has updated its[2024 Billion-dollar disaster analysis](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/billions "(opens in a new window)"). In 2024, there were 27 individual weather and climate disasters with at least $1 billion in damages, trailing only the record-setting 28 events analyzed in 2023. These disasters caused at least **568 direct or indirect fatalities,**which is the eighth-highest for these billion-dollar disasters over the last 45 years (1980-2024). The cost was approximately $182.7 billion.

This total places 2024 as the fourth-costliest on record, trailing 2017 ($395.9 billion), 2005 ($268.5 billion) and 2022 ($183.6 billion). Adding the 27 events of 2024 to the record that begins in 1980, the U.S. has sustained**403 weather and climate disasters**for which the individual damage costs reached or exceeded $1 billion. The cumulative cost for these 403 events exceeds**$2.915 trillion**.

Before presenting the analysis of 2024, here are a few notes for additional context. This research is a quantification of the weather and climate disasters that in 2024 led to more than $1 billion in collective damages for each event, and all prior-year cost estimates are adjusted for inflation to 2024 dollars using the Consumer Price Index. Additionally, these cost totals for 2024 are based on analysis through January 10 th, 2025, and may rise an additional several billion dollars, as new data become available. This analysis is conservative, as it excludes events with less than $1 billion in damages in 2024 dollars. However, it does include 57 events since 1980 that were originally below the billion-dollar threshold but are now above $1 billion in 2024 dollars.

The billion-dollar disasters of 2024 came from multiple categories:

*   2 winter storm/cold wave events (across the Northwest and central/southern U.S. in mid-January).
*   1 wildfire event (the South Fork Fire in New Mexico that destroyed many homes, vehicles, businesses and other infrastructure).
*   1 drought and heat wave event (causing impacts across the southern, eastern and northwestern U.S.).
*   1 flooding event (the Upper Midwest Flooding in mid-June across several states).
*   6 tornado outbreaks (across the central and southeastern U.S.).
*   5 tropical cyclones (Beryl, Debby, Francine, Helene and Milton – the final two were the costliest U.S. disasters of 2024).
*   11 severe weather/hail events (across many parts of the country).

Costliest U.S. billion-dollar disasters of 2024
-----------------------------------------------

Among the many weather and climate-related disasters to affect the U.S. in 2024, the following caused the most damaging impacts and heavily impacted many communities.

### Hurricane Helene, September 24-29: 219 deaths, $79.6 billion

Category 4 Hurricane Helene with 140 mph sustained winds was the strongest hurricane on record to strike the Big Bend region of Florida having made landfall near Perry, Florida on September 26. Helene was the third hurricane to hit the Big Bend region in just over a year. It caused up to 15 feet of storm surge along the Big Bend coast and six feet of surge as far south as St. Petersburg. It also caused billions of dollars in damage to Georgia's agriculture sector. Helene's most severe impacts were from the historic rainfall (up to 30+ inches) and flooding across much of western North Carolina.

[![Image 4: destroyed railroad bridge and mud-covered streets in Asheville, NC](https://www.climate.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_width_stretch_featured_image/public/2025-01/helene_asheville-railroad-bridge-adam-smith.png?itok=HZAb_E1j)](https://www.climate.gov/media/16726)

This photo from September 29, 2024, shows a damaged railroad bridge and mud-covered streets in Asheville, North Carolina, following flooding on the Swannanoa River during Hurricane Helene. Photo by Adam B. Smith.

This flooding eclipsed the region's previous worst flood from 1916. Asheville and many surrounding cities and communities were heavily impacted. Southwestern Virginia and extreme eastern Tennessee were also heavily impacted. Damage came in many forms. Landslides, debris flows, and historic levels of flooding inundated and destroyed homes, businesses, parks, hospitals, the electrical, cellular and water system infrastructure, and damaged thousands of roads, highways and bridges, as examples. Additional information is currently being assembled that summarizes the vast scope of damage produced by Helene. Helene was the deadliest Atlantic hurricane since Maria (2017), and the deadliest to strike the U.S. mainland since Katrina (2005).

[![Image 5: Map of Southeast United States showing that the chances for 3-day rainfall in excess of the amounts observed during Hurricane Helene were extremely low](https://www.climate.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_width_620_original_image/public/2024-11/hurricane-helene-rainfall-exceedance-probabilities-2000px.jpg?itok=FXWL7gWR)](https://www.climate.gov/media/16581)

Between September 23 and 28, the highest 3-day rainfall totals across the higher elevations of the southern Appalachian Mountains were so extreme that the statistical chances of them being exceeded in an any given year were 1 in 1,000 (black areas). Statistically, this is the same as saying that averaged over long periods of time, a 3-day rainfall event so extreme would only occur on average (not literally!) once every 1,000 years. NOAA Climate.gov graphic, adapted from original by NOAA's [National Weather Service](https://www.weather.gov/media/owp/oh/hdsc/docs/202409_Helene.pdf "(opens in a new window)").

### Hurricane Milton, October 9-10: 32 deaths, $34.3 billion

Category 3 Hurricane Milton with 120 mph sustained winds made landfall near Siesta Key, Florida on October 9. A storm surge of 5 to 10 feet caused damage from Naples to Charlotte Harbor. Milton's track to the south of Tampa Bay lessened storm surge impacts on the densely populated Tampa metro region.

[![Image 6: Florida Fish and Wildlife officer carries child on her shoulders through shin-deep water in a flooded street](https://www.climate.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_width_620_original_image/public/2025-01/billion-dollar-disasters-milton-Florida-Fish-Wildlife-officer-evacuates-child.jpg?itok=Sz5LITl2)](https://www.climate.gov/media/16725)

A Florida Fish and Wildlife officer helps to evacuate a flooded Florida neighborhood in the aftermath of Hurricane Milton on October 10, 2024. Florida Fish and Wildlife [photo from their Flickr account.](https://www.flickr.com/photos/myfwcmedia/54064908259/ "(opens in a new window)") Image has been cropped and brightened. Used under a Creative Commons [license.](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/2.0/ "(opens in a new window)")

Dozens of tornadoes were also spawned from Milton, damaging homes, businesses, vehicles and other infrastructure across southern Florida. Milton underwent rapid intensification into a Category 5 hurricane with 180 mph sustained winds and a 897 mb central pressure reading. An environment of enhanced wind shear the day prior to landfall reduced Milton's peak wind potential.

### Hurricane Beryl, July 8: 46 deaths, $7.2 billion

[![Image 7: Satellite image of Hurricane Beryl July 1, 2024 facebook promo](https://www.climate.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_width_620_original_image/public/2024-07/event-tracker-hurricane-beryl-fb-share-1200x630%20%282%29.png?itok=5e2s7bEj)](https://www.climate.gov/media/16196)

GOES-16 satellite imagery of Hurricane Beryl on July 1, 2024, as it rapidly intensified in the Caribbean Sea. Hours after this image was taken, Beryl strengthened into a catastrophic Category 5 hurricane. Credit: CSU/CIRA & NOAA

Category 1 Hurricane Beryl made landfall in Texas on July 8, producing widespread high wind damage, as the storm was re-strengthening at landfall. One significant impact was power outages that impacted millions of people for days. Beryl also produced more than 50 tornadoes across eastern Texas, western Louisiana and southern Arkansas. On July 1, Beryl became the earliest Category 5 hurricane and the second Category 5 on record during the month of July in the Atlantic Ocean.

### Central, southern & southeast tornado outbreak, May 6-9: 3 deaths, $6.6 billion

An outbreak producing more than 165 tornadoes developed across many central, southern and southeastern states. The states most affected include Oklahoma, Kansas, Nebraska, Michigan, Indiana, Ohio, Kentucky, Tennessee, Alabama, North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia and Florida. This multi-day tornado outbreak produced at least 61 EF-0, 79 EF-1, 13 EF-2, three EF-3, one EF-4 tornado and dozens of EF-U (unknown/unrated) tornadoes, causing widespread damage to homes, businesses, vehicles, agriculture and other infrastructure. The towns of Barnsdall and Bartlesville, Oklahoma, were impacted by an EF-4 tornado that caused extensive damage.

2024 costs in historical context
--------------------------------

The year 2024 (red line) is the 14 th-consecutive year (2011-2024) in which 10 or more separate billion-dollar disaster events have impacted the U.S. The 1980–2023 annual average (black line) is 9.0 events; the annual average for the most recent 5 years (2020–2024) is 23.0 events.

[![Image 8: Line graph showing monthly cumulative billion-dollar disaster costs for all years 1980-2024](https://www.climate.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_width_stretch_featured_image/public/2025-01/NOAA-billion-dollar-disasters-counts-by-month-1980-2024.png?itok=V31iQbef)](https://www.climate.gov/media/16721)

Month-by-month accumulation of billion-dollar disasters for each year on record. The colored lines represent the top 6 years for most billion-dollar disasters. The dark gray line shows the average. All other years are colored light gray. NOAA NCEI Billion-dollar Disasters[webpage.](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/billions/ "(opens in a new window)")

Over the last ten years (2015-2024), the U.S. has been impacted by 190 separate billion-dollar disasters that have killed more than 6,300 people (direct and indirect fatalities) and cost ~$1.4 trillion in damage. The U.S. mainland has been impacted by landfalling Category 4 or 5 hurricanes in six of the last eight years, including Hurricanes Harvey, Irma, Maria, Michael, Laura, Ida, Ian and Helene. The impacts from Hurricanes Helene and Milton were particularly destructive, causing more than $100 billion in combined damage across Florida, Georgia, South Carolina, North Carolina, Tennessee, and Virginia over a two-week period from late-September into early October.

[![Image 9: Line graph showing monthly cumulative costs of billion-dollar disasters](https://www.climate.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_width_stretch_featured_image/public/2025-01/NOAA-billion-dollar-disasters-costs-by-month-1980-2024.png?itok=vUr3ycOL)](https://www.climate.gov/media/16716)

Month-by-month accumulation of estimated costs of each year's billion-dollar disasters, with colored lines showing 2024 (red) and the other top-10 costliest years. Other years are light gray. 2024 finished the year in fourth place for annual costs. Screenshot from the NOAA NCEI Billion-dollar Disasters[webpage.](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/billions/ "(opens in a new window)")

The total cost of U.S. billion-dollar disasters over the last 5 years (2020-2024) is**$746.7 billion**, with a 5-year annual cost average of**$149.3 billion**. This 5-year cost average is more than double the 45-year (1980-2024) annual cost average of $64.8 billion for all of the billion-dollar disaster events.

Increasing trend of disasters: exposure, vulnerability, and influence of climate change
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

It is clear that the number of billion-dollars disasters and their total costs have risen since 1980.Even though the analysis adjusts for inflation, none of the highest years are from before 2000. The graph below shows it more clearly.

[![Image 10: Combination graphic showing counts of billion-dollar disasters and costs each year 1980-2024](https://www.climate.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_width_stretch_featured_image/public/2025-01/NOAA-billion-dollar-disasters-counts-versus-total-costs-1980-2024.png?itok=tjjvBZlE)](https://www.climate.gov/media/16723)

The history of billion-dollar disasters in the United States each year from 1980 to 2024, showing event type (colors), frequency (left-hand vertical axis), and cost (right-hand vertical axis) adjusted for inflation to 2024 dollars. NOAA NCEI Billion-dollar Disasters[webpage.](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/billions/ "(opens in a new window)")

Losses from the billion-dollar disasters tracked by NCEI have averaged $140 billion per year over the last decade (NCEI, 2025). 2017 was the costliest year, exceeding $300 billion—proportional to about 25% of the $1.3 trillion building value put in place that year (Multi-Hazard Mitigation Council, 2019, [full report](https://www.nibs.org/projects/natural-hazard-mitigation-saves-2019-report "(opens in a new window)")).

The cost per capita (see right y-axis in chart below) has also remained at a high level for the U.S. as a whole since 2017 when compared to previous years even after adjusting for CPI-inflation. This indicates that the costs of the billion-dollar disasters are rising more sharply than general population growth. The chart shows the 5-year-average disaster cost per capita was about $150 (inflation-adjusted) per U.S. resident in the early-2000s. The 5-year average disaster cost per capita then increased above $400 per person in the late 2010s and has remained at a high level in recent years.

[![Image 11: Combination graph with bar chart showing disaster counts on left axis and cost per capita on the right axis](https://www.climate.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_width_stretch_featured_image/public/2025-01/NOAA-billion-dollar-disasters-counts-versus-per-capita-costs-1980-2024.png?itok=vi4f2CMf)](https://www.climate.gov/media/16722)

The history of billion-dollar disasters in the United States each year from 1980 to 2024, showing event type (colors), frequency (left-hand vertical axis), and total cost (right-hand vertical axis) adjusted for inflation using the Consumer Price Index.NOAA NCEI Billion-dollar Disasters[webpage.](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/billions/ "(opens in a new window)")

There are several potential explanations for these trends, including increases in[exposure](https://content.naic.org/consumer_glossary.htm#E "(opens in a new window)")(i.e., more assets at risk); increases in[vulnerability](https://www.naic.org/documents/cipr_study_1704_flood_risk.pdf "(opens in a new window)")(i.e., the amount of damage a hazard of given intensity, such as high winds, can cause at a location); and increases in the frequency and intensity of some types of extreme events due to human-caused climate change ([National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine. Compounding Disasters in Gulf Coast Communities 2020-2021: Impacts, Findings, and Lessons (2024)](https://www.nationalacademies.org/news/2024/07/intensifying-pace-and-severity-of-extreme-events-increases-risks-of-compounding-disasters-and-demands-rethinking-of-u-s-emergency-management-says-new-report "(opens in a new window)"); [Multi-Hazard Mitigation Council. Natural Hazard Mitigation Saves (2019)](https://www.nibs.org/files/pdfs/NIBS_MMC_MitigationSaves_2019.pdf "(opens in a new window)"); [Fifth U.S. National Climate Assessment (2023](https://nca2023.globalchange.gov/ "(opens in a new window)")).

A major driver of increased costs of extreme weather is the increase in population and material wealth over the last several decades. For example, in an analysis of recent disasters in the Gulf of Mexico region, the National Academies concluded, “Exposure to extreme weather and climate hazards in the Gulf of Mexico state region is increasing as a result of population growth and increased construction of public and private development in hazard-prone areas" (National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine. [Compounding Disasters in Gulf Coast Communities 2020-2021: Impacts, Findings, and Lessons](https://nap.nationalacademies.org/catalog/27170/compounding-disasters-in-gulf-coast-communities-2020-2021-impacts-findings "(opens in a new window)"), 2024).

Much of this growth and property development has continued in high-risk areas like coasts, the fire-prone wildland-urban interface in the West, and river floodplains. Increased building and population growth in these high-risk areas mean that more people and property are at risk and so also contribute to larger losses (CBO report “[Climate Change, Disaster Risk, and Homeowner’s Insurance](https://www.cbo.gov/publication/59918 "(opens in a new window)"),” 2024). Areas where building codes are insufficient for reducing damage from extreme events are especially vulnerable to more expensive extreme weather.

Population growth and how and where we build play a big role in the increasing number and costs of billion-dollar disasters. But we also know from [extreme event attribution](https://www.climate.gov/news-features/understanding-climate/extreme-event-attribution-climate-versus-weather-blame-game) research that human-caused climate change is[increasing the frequency](https://www.climate.gov/news-features/event-tracker/global-warming-increased-risk-intensity-louisianas-extreme-rain-event)and intensity of certain types of extreme weather that lead to billion-dollar disasters—most notably the rise in vulnerability to drought, lengthening wildfire seasons in the Western states, and the potential for extremely heavy rainfall becoming more common in the eastern states. Sea level rise is worsening hurricane[storm surge flooding](https://www.climate.gov/news-features/features/superstorm-sandy-and-sea-level-rise). (Read more about changes in climate and weather extremes in the[Fifth U.S. National Climate Assessment (2023)](https://nca2023.globalchange.gov/ "(opens in a new window)").Given those trends, it’s likely that human-caused climate change is having some level of influence on the rising costs of billion-dollar disasters.

Some mix of these factors is likely the reason that the 2010s decade was far costlier in the Billion-Dollar Disaster data set than the 2000s, 1990s, or 1980s, even when adjusted for inflation to current dollars). See the table below.

[![Image 12: Screenshot of table showing billion-dollar disaster costs sorted by decade](https://www.climate.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_width_stretch_featured_image/public/2025-01/NOAA-billion-dollar-disasters-summary-stats-table-by-decade_1980-2024.png?itok=4KdFJ6ve)](https://www.climate.gov/media/16714)

This table shows summary statistics of billion-dollar disasters by decade and by latest 1, 3-, and 5-year periods. Costs over the last complete decade, the 5-year average, and the 3-year average are all higher than the costs of the 1980s, 1990s, and 2000s. Screenshot from NOAA NCEI Billion-dollar Disasters[webpage.](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/billions/ "(opens in a new window)")

1980-2024 U.S. costs and fatalities by disaster type
----------------------------------------------------

Tropical cyclones caused the most damage from 1980 to 2024 ($1,543.2 billion) and had the highest average event cost ($23.0 billion per event). Severe storms ($514.3 billion), drought ($367.5 billion), and inland flooding ($203.0 billion) also caused considerable damage. Severe storms account for the highest number of billion-dollar disaster events (203), but they have the lowest average event cost ($2.5 billion), which is consistent with their localized nature. Tropical cyclones and flooding represent the second and third most frequent event types (67 and 45), respectively. Tropical cyclones are responsible for the highest number of deaths (7,211), followed by drought/heatwave events (4,658) and severe storms (2,145).

[![Image 13: Table showing summary stats of billion-dollar disasters by event type](https://www.climate.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_width_stretch_featured_image/public/2025-01/NOAA-billion-dollar-disasters-summary-stats-table-1980-2024.png?itok=88RDhAiw)](https://www.climate.gov/media/16715)

This table shows the breakdown, by hazard type, of the 403 billion-dollar weather and climate disasters assessed since 1980. Severe storms are far and away the most frequent type of billion-dollar disaster. Screenshot from the NOAA NCEI Billion-dollar Disasters[webpage.](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/billions/ "(opens in a new window)")

enriched_points.expanded_content: From 1970 to 2021, extreme weather events led to reported economic losses exceeding $4.3 trillion globally, highlighting the escalating financial impact of climate-related disasters.

Regions including Southeast Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa, and parts of Europe are particularly vulnerable, suffering disproportionately high economic losses from climate-related events and exacerbating existing inequalities.
Agriculture has been notably affected, with droughts and floods causing substantial crop losses, leading to food insecurity and economic instability in dependent communities.

Climate-induced disasters have resulted in significant infrastructure damage, with costs estimated at hundreds of billions annually, straining public resources and necessitating urgent repairs and upgrades.
Recovery from climate disasters is increasingly costly, with the average U.S. billion-dollar weather disaster costing $182.7 billion in 2024 alone, indicating rising recovery expenditures.
